filename: How to make a great poster.docx
relative_path: Guidelines/Research-related/Abstracts and presentations/How to make a great poster.docx
full_text:
Here are some tips about how to make a nice poster according to my experience

"Conference attendees will look at your poster only briefly, so a clear presentation is crucial" [2]

Firstly, planning is critical! Leave enough time to prepare your poster. The first time - it will take longer than you expected.

Plan the schedule:

1) Check where and when you will send it for printing

2) Leave at least 1 week for feedback and edits. Check with Anniina and potentially your other project members and collaborators when they can give comments to the poster.

This is also critical to make sure to present the correct affiliations, external collaborators and funding.

3) When you are done - A good tip - ask your friend, colleague to do a final grammarly/overall check for mistakes before printing.

After you have finished the poster - save and share the abstract and poster in datacloud: https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/f/1018895

Name your poster and abstract files: Project_Yourlastname_Conference_Date in the format of NKI_Perez_AACROvarian2023_20231002

1. Choose a nice software to make the poster

I recommend inkscape: https://inkscape.org/

Pros:

It allows you to make SVG files.

All the objects never change/move, are just the way you add them.

It has hundreds of options available, like how to adjust colors, align all text.

Once you know how to use it, it saves you so much time.

Cons:

It takes so much time to learn how to use it

It is a bit complex to use, and not intuitive as power-point.

The second I recommend is Microsoft Publisher.

I have done all my posters in Microsof PowerPoint (@aemoksan@helsinki_fi )

It has all the necessary features and is easy to use

Be careful when you import images from other software- they may loose resolution especially if you copy-paste. Paste special as either tiff or pdf usually overcomes this issue.

I do not recommend: Microsoft Word, Biorender, because all the text and figures are always moving whenever you are editing any section.

2. Identify a nice template or poster structure

Check other posters made by the Lab-Members you can find those in the G-Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1CJ_cpKOdLEwb4cv0lDGr1yLKBEkq5eMN?usp=share_link

and in the Datacloud: https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/apps/files/?dir=/farkkila/Presentations_posters_abstracts&fileid=1018895

Check the further reading materials to get to know nice poster structures.

3. Text and font

Make sure to not add so much text, only the indispensable one.

For your figures, make sure the legends and axis-names are large enough to be seen far away.

For the methods, if possible create a figure or couple of figures describing the methods instead of adding text.

4. Your Figures

Your poster must contain mostly figures. Add the figures more relevant related to your project. A good number could be around 10 figures.

Make sure your figures are clear, with axis fonts large enough.

You can check the next tutorials about how to make nice scientific figures:

"How to Make Good Graphs and Figures for Scientific Papers": https://www.simplifiedsciencepublishing.com/resources/how-to-make-good-graphs-and-figures-for-scientific-papers

"Ten simple rules for better figures":https://doi.org/10.1136/sbmj.f297

Ask your colleagues if your figures are informative. Every figure should be really clear and people need to understand the main point in around a minute.

Try to include one big eye-catching Figure that attracts people to your poster. For example, a colorful tCycif image, or a colorful graphical abstract (like those in the cell journal's articles).

Add always figure numbers (or letters) to each one of your figures.

Each figure should have a note/legend of the result you are showing in the figure. Example: "A) Higher immune infiltration after treatment X". Add this always on the top of each figure.

5. Avoid empty spaces

Fill all possible space with your figures, do not leave any empty space in your poster.

6. Make sure to check the correct affiliations, and funding references

Include logos of the funding agencies - Check these with Anniina and all co-authors.

ADD YOUR CONTACT INFORMATION TO THE POSTER (email, X-account)

Some Important information on image quality for the poster

If an image has low DPI (e.g., 72 DPI from screenshots or other), it will look blurry when printed large (A0 poster). So increasing the DPI will give us sharper and detailed images.

How to check DPI of an image? (Mac)

1. Open the image in Preview.

2. Click Tools in the menu bar.

3. Select Show Inspector (Command + I).

4. In the Inspector window, click on the "i" (Info) tab.

5. Look for Resolution—it will say something like 72 DPI, 300 DPI, or 600 DPI.

If the DPI is below 300, you need to increase it manually for better print quality.

Note that, only increasing DPI doesn’t improve quality—it only changes metadata. So the best fix is to increase both image size and DPI.

Step 1: Upscale Your Images Using BigJPG

Go to BigJPG.com.

Upload your image → Click ‘Start’

Choose ‘Photo’ mode → Set 4x upscale → Click OK.

Wait for processing → Download the upscaled image.

Despite this, if your image has not changed the DPI, you can manually do this.

Step 2: Adjust DPI in Preview

Open the image in Preview.

Click Tools → Adjust Size.

Uncheck “Resample Image” (Important!).

Change DPI to 600.

Click OK → Save the file.

- Further reading

Here are some nice tutorials about how to make a nice poster.

"Making a better research poster": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AwMFhyH7_5g

"How to make a scientific poster": https://doi.org/10.1136/sbmj.f297
